statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Alice and Bob are playing a game. They have an array $ a $ of $ n $ elements and an array $ b $ of $ m $ elements.

The players take turns. Alice goes first. On their turn, each player chooses a number $ x $ from array $ a $ and a number $ y $ from array $ b $ . Alice has her own rule for choosing $ x $ and $ y $ , and Bob has his:

- Alice must choose $ x $ and $ y $ such that $ y $ is divisible by $ x $ .
- Bob must choose $ x $ and $ y $ such that $ y $ is not divisible by $ x $ .

After choosing $ x $ and $ y $ , $ y $ is removed from the array $ b $ (but $ x $ remains in $ a $ ). When $ y $ is removed from $ b $ , if there are multiple occurrences of $ y $ , only one is removed. The player who cannot make a move loses.

Who will win if both players play optimally?

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains two integers $ n $ and $ m $ ( $ 1 \le n, m \le 10^{6} $ ).

The second line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ a_{i} $ ( $ 1 \le a_{i} \le n + m $ ) — the elements of the array $ a $ .

The last line of each test case contains $ m $ integers $ b_{i} $ ( $ 1 \le b_{i} \le n + m $ ) — the elements of the array $ b $ .

Additional constraints on the input:

- the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 10^6 $ ;
- the sum of $ m $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 10^6 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, print one word:

- Alice if Alice wins;
- Bob if Bob wins.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
3
9 3
3 2 4 2 2 4 4 2 4
6 7 12
10 3
3 2 5 4 2 5 3 4 4 4
10 7 13
1 5
1
1 2 3 4 5
Output
Alice
Bob
Alice

Note
Consider the first test case. Let's show how Alice wins through the moves:

- Alice's move: $ x = 3, y = 6 $ (after this, $ 6 $ will be removed from $ b $ , resulting in $ b = [7, 12] $ )
- Bob's move: $ x = 3, y = 7 $ (Bob will choose $ y = 7 $ on his turn anyway, since there is no $ x $ that does not divide $ y = 12 $ , after this move $ b = [12] $ )
- Alice's move: $ x = 4, y = 12 $ (after this move, $ b $ becomes empty)
- Bob's move: Bob loses, as he cannot make a move

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2203D - Divisibility Game
We will divide the numbers from the array $$$b$$$ into three classes:
Numbers that can only be removed by Alice (they are divisible by every number in the array $$$a$$$), we will call them Alice's numbers;
Numbers that can only be removed by Bob (none of the numbers in the array $$$a$$$ divide them evenly), we will call them Bob's numbers;
Numbers that can be removed by both Alice and Bob, we will call them common numbers.
It is easy to guess that the optimal strategy for each player will be to first remove the common numbers and only then remove their own.
The main difficulty of the problem is to learn how to quickly determine to which class each number from $$$b$$$ belongs.
Let's take an arbitrary number from $$$b$$$ and denote it as $$$y$$$. We will try to check whether it is Bob's number or Alice's number; if it is neither, then it is a common number.
Let's look at all the divisors of the number $$$y$$$. For $$$y$$$ to be Alice's number, the array $$$a$$$ must consist only of its divisors, that is:
$$$$$$\displaystyle\sum_{d|y} \text{cnt}[d] = n$$$$$$
where $$$\text{cnt}[d]$$$ denotes the number of occurrences of the number $$$d$$$ in the array $$$a$$$.
Similarly, for $$$y$$$ to be Bob's number, there should not be any of its divisors in the array $$$a$$$:
$$$$$$\displaystyle\sum_{d|y} \text{cnt}[d] = 0$$$$$$
Now we can already write a solution that finds all the divisors of the number and directly checks these two conditions. However, this is slow and works in $$$O(\sqrt{(n + m)} \cdot m)$$$.
Instead of checking each number from $$$b$$$ separately, let's try to calculate an array $$$c$$$ such that:
$$$$$$c_{i} = \displaystyle\sum_{d|i} \text{cnt}[d]$$$$$$
How can we do this? With a simple nested loop:
for(int i = 1; i <= n + m; i++){
for (int j = 1; i * j <= n + m; j++){
c[i * j] += cnt[i];
}
}
In this loop, we iterate over the number $$$i$$$ and add $$$\text{cnt}[i]$$$ to all numbers that are divisible by it. After that, using the array $$$c$$$, we can check the class of a number in $$$O(1)$$$.
This works faster than iterating over all divisors of each number. Let's estimate the number of operations of these nested loops: for $$$i = 1$$$, the inner loop will make $$$n + m$$$ iterations; for $$$i = 2$$$: $$$\frac{n + m}{2}$$$; for $$$i = 3$$$: $$$\frac{n + m}{3}$$$, and so on. Thus, the total number of operations is:
$$$$$$\displaystyle\sum_{i = 1}^{n + m} \frac{n + m}{i} = (n + m) \cdot \displaystyle\sum_{i = 1}^{n + m} \frac{1}{i}$$$$$$
The last factor is the
harmonic series
. It is known that asymptotically it is equal to $$$\ln (n + m) + \gamma$$$, where $$$\gamma$$$ is a constant, so the number of operations of the nested loops, and thus the entire problem, is solved in $$$O((n + m) \cdot \log (n + m))$$$.

solutions.title: Solution (FelixArg)
